primary_diagnosis.name: Acute unilateral lower-limb cellulitis with systemic inflammatory response

pathophysiology_summary: Bacteria (commonly Streptococcus/Staphylococcus) have entered through a break in the skin (insect bite) and spread through the dermis and subcutaneous tissue, producing hot, tender, poorly demarcated, rapidly spreading unilateral erythema with oedema (peau d'orange) and tender regional lymphadenopathy, now with a systemic inflammatory response (fever, rigors, tachycardia). Management is systemic anti-streptococcal/anti-staphylococcal antibiotics, limb ELEVATION (which — unlike in arterial ulcers — reduces oedema and speeds healing), analgesia and border monitoring, with urgent in-person assessment because of the systemic features. The must-not-miss diagnoses are necrotising fasciitis (pain out of proportion, dusky/violaceous skin or bullae, crepitus, anaesthesia, rapid progression, systemic toxicity) — a surgical emergency — and DVT; here necrotising features are screened negative and the febrile demarcated erythema with a clear portal of entry favours cellulitis over DVT and over chronic bilateral stasis dermatitis.

differential.evidence_basis: Unilateral spreading hot tender erythema from a bite, with fever/rigors.
A surgical emergency to exclude in any rapidly spreading painful limb infection with systemic toxicity.
Swollen painful leg; fever, demarcated erythema and a portal of entry favour infection but DVT should be considered.
Chronic, bilateral, non-febrile — inconsistent with this acute unilateral febrile picture.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise cellulitis with SIRS, screen for necrotising fasciitis/sepsis, and arrange urgent in-person antibiotics; advise ELEVATION (not dependency).

differential.evidence_basis: Classic cellulitis; necrotising features screened negative, favouring cellulitis needing urgent antibiotics.
Currently no pain-out-of-proportion/bullae/crepitus/anaesthesia, but remains the critical safety-net if features evolve.
Acute unilateral febrile onset excludes chronic bilateral stasis dermatitis.
Working diagnosis: urgent same-day in-person antibiotics (oral vs IV per severity), elevation, analgesia, border monitoring, with a necrotising-fasciitis/sepsis safety-net.

red_flags.description: Systemic inflammatory response (fever, rigors, tachycardia) — sepsis risk
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic toxicity indicates significant infection and possible sepsis; supports urgent in-person assessment and possible IV antibiotics.

red_flags.description: Necrotising fasciitis features: pain out of proportion, dusky/violaceous skin or haemorrhagic bullae, crepitus, anaesthesia, very rapid progression
red_flags.clinical_significance: A surgical emergency with high mortality; must be actively excluded.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed necrotising fasciitis is rapidly fatal without urgent surgery.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating febrile spreading cellulitis as trivial with routine follow-up instead of urgent in-person assessment.
Not screening for pain-out-of-proportion, bullae, crepitus or anaesthesia.
Advising dependency; cellulitis benefits from ELEVATION to reduce oedema.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for crepitus / pain out of proportion
Marking and tracking the erythema border
Assessing haemodynamics and for sepsis objectively
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day in-person assessment for systemic antibiotics (oral vs IV per severity) and sepsis screening; ED/ambulance if sepsis or necrotising features. Elevate the limb.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Managing febrile spreading cellulitis remotely without in-person assessment, or missing necrotising fasciitis.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Treat cellulitis with anti-streptococcal/anti-staphylococcal antibiotics; elevate the limb; use IV therapy and admission for systemic toxicity/failure of oral therapy; exclude necrotising infection.
